title: Process substrate — shape contract (COR-033)
description: Shared shape for a process DEFINITION a capability authors. Capability instance schemas cross-file $ref these $defs (e.g. "_defs/process.schema.json#/$defs/process"). Per the .pkit/process/ area spec + COR-033. Ship-narrow: enums admit only the implemented core values (singleton + keyed cardinality, inferred detection); remaining deferred values widen the enum when they ship. additionalProperties is permissive on the extensible objects (process, state, transition) so a capability's own domain-local fields coexist; tight on the core sub-objects.

predicate.description: A check the engine evaluates by running a capability-provided command (COR-033 P4). The command returns structured JSON the engine reads: deterministic -> {result, reason, detail?}; authorisation-artifact -> {exists, produced_by, reason, detail?}.

run.description: Name of a command the owning capability registers (its scripts/ + package.yaml). The engine runs it with the subject + context.

with.description: Optional static arguments passed to the command.

blocked.description: An orthogonal, optional first-class WAIT declaration on a subject (COR-034). NOT a definition state -- it adds no state to the process and cannot explode the state space. It names what the subject waits ON; RESUME differs by reason. For awaiting-human there is NO resume predicate -- the resume IS the person taking the pending move (position advancing off the parked state), and the engine consults no side-predicate. For awaiting-condition the block carries a `resume_when` predicate the engine re-evaluates LIVE and auto-clears when it holds (no human in the loop). The current blocked-ness is always the live evaluation, authoritative over any journal entry (inheriting COR-033's journal-is-intent-log / live-detection-authoritative contract); `since`/`assignee` are the audit colour journaled on enter. additionalProperties false: the shipped wait shape is tight.

blocked_on.description: Why the subject waits -- a reason establishable from ONE subject's reality (COR-032), except awaiting-cascade-outcome which is the single sanctioned cross-subject FOLD wait (COR-037). awaiting-human: a person must act; the resume IS the person taking the pending move (no `resume_when` -- it is forbidden, because a side-predicate could disagree with the move while it is still gate-closed). awaiting-condition: an external fact must become true; carries a `resume_when` predicate (required) that turns true on its own, no human in the loop. awaiting-subprocess-outcome (COR-036, single-inner): the subject is parked in a `subprocess` state whose embedded inner process has not yet reached a wired terminal outcome; auto-clearing like awaiting-condition (the 'condition' is 'the inner reached outcome X', re-evaluated LIVE by the recursive resolution), so it carries NO `resume_when` (the resolution IS its condition). awaiting-cascade-outcome (COR-037, the AGGREGATE wait): the subject is parked at a state whose outgoing `cascade-outcome` gate folds across the members of one declared child process and has not yet resolved open; auto-clearing like awaiting-subprocess-outcome (the 'condition' IS the live fold), so it carries NO `resume_when`. The remaining cross-subject reason (deadlock -- a peer-subject cycle) stays deferred per COR-034; this enum is open to further additive widening.

resume_when.description: The predicate the engine re-evaluates LIVE (the same predicate runner that backs detection and gates). REQUIRED for awaiting-condition and FORBIDDEN for awaiting-human (enforced by the if/then below): when it holds the subject is no longer blocked and the engine auto-clears the flag. awaiting-human carries no resume_when -- its resume is the person taking the pending move, tracked by position advancing off the parked state, never a side-predicate.

assignee.description: Optional: who owns the wait (the stuck-work view's owner). Content-free -- the engine carries it, the binding interprets it.

allOf.description: COR-034: resume differs by reason. awaiting-condition REQUIRES a `resume_when` (the external fact the engine re-checks live); awaiting-human FORBIDS one (its resume is the person taking the move, never a side-predicate -- a satisfied side-predicate while the move is still gate-closed would falsely report 'not waiting'). Mirrors the prompt-on-user-only enforcement on transitions.

subject.description: What the process tracks the position of.

cardinality.description: singleton: one journey per process (no subject id; the engine uses a fixed key). keyed (COR-032): many units under one definition, each at its own position -- the engine operates per a supplied subject identifier (required for keyed; no singleton default) and never enumerates. Cross-subject enumeration/cascade remain deferred breadth.

key.description: Descriptive only (COR-032): names what identifies a keyed unit (e.g. `issue-number`). The engine does not interpret it; supplied subject identifiers must be safe to use in the per-subject journal path.

domain_ref.description: Pointer to where the subject's domain data lives -- kept distinct from its process position.

blocked.description: Optional, additive (COR-034): the subject's wait declaration. Absent on every existing process -- those validate byte-unchanged.

detection.description: How the engine resolves whether a subject is in a state.

mode.description: Ship-narrow: position inferred from reality via the predicate. `stored`/`hybrid` widen this enum when they ship.

depends_on.description: COR-038: ONE inert, schema-validated cross-process connection a state declares (the entries live in the `depends_on` LIST on a state). INERT by design -- the engine's runtime (`status`/`can-move`/`move`/`validate`-of-position) NEVER reads it: its only readers are the SCHEMA (shape-validates it here) and a future render (out-of-engine, draws the topology). So a malformed entry is a definition-authoring LINT error, never a fail-closed gate -- it cannot affect whether any subject may move. It annotates ONLY the edges the engine cannot already see; an enforced `composed-subprocess` / `aggregates` edge is fully declared by the `subprocess`/`cascade` block the engine owns, so the render DERIVES those (derive-don't-annotate, single-source-of-truth COR-006) and they are deliberately NOT relation values here.

upstream.description: The upstream process address `<capability>:<process-id>` (the same address grammar `subprocess`/`cascade` use). The render reads it to draw the edge; the engine never resolves it.

relation.description: COR-038: the CLOSED set of exactly the connections the engine cannot otherwise see. informational: advisory, no runtime effect. gates-on-readiness: names the cross-process edge an opaque gate predicate enforces but declares nowhere. triggered-by: an externally/connector-mediated coupling the engine never sees (pairs with `mode: push`). constrained-with: a cross-subject invariant named now for visibility, enforcement deferred behind COR-035's cross-subject-invariants slot. `composed-subprocess`/`aggregates` are DELIBERATELY ABSENT -- those edges are derived from `subprocess`/`cascade`, never annotated (drift would make the trusted render lie).

mode.description: COR-038: pull = a connection the reader resolves on its own turn by detection over reality. push = mediated OUTSIDE the engine (an external tool / out-of-engine binding glue) -- it introduces NO eventing into the position engine (which stays pull-only, COR-038 point 3); it is recorded SOLELY for visibility.

why.description: COR-038: the required human-readable reason the render surfaces -- why this connection exists. The same `why` convention detection/gates/blocked/invariants carry.

state.description: A node. additionalProperties permitted for capability-local fields (e.g. applies_to).

meaning.description: Load-bearing: the status view renders from it.

subprocess.description: COR-036 (optional, additive): marks this state as embedding an inner process. While the subject is parked here, the engine recursively resolves the inner process's terminal outcome and the parent's outgoing `subprocess-outcome` gates read it. Absent on every existing state -- those validate byte-unchanged.

depends_on.description: COR-038 (optional, additive): INERT cross-process connection metadata -- a list of declared connections to upstream processes that the engine NEVER evaluates (only the schema shape-validates them, only a future render reads them to draw the topology). Absent on every existing state -- those validate byte-unchanged. Carries ONLY the edges the engine cannot see; composition/aggregation edges are derived from `subprocess`/`cascade` instead (derive-don't-annotate).

entry.description: Marks a start state. `true` = unconditional; an object with `when` = guarded entry (selected when the predicate holds; multiple guarded entries allowed).

terminal.description: End state; also a process OUTCOME for composition (COR-036) -- a parent reads which terminal state an embedded inner process reached.

gate.description: What must hold for a move. Always reduces to a checkable, deterministic answer (COR-033 P4). Most kinds reduce to a capability `predicate`; the `subprocess-outcome` kind (COR-036) is computed by the ENGINE from an embedded inner process's resolved terminal outcome, and the `cascade-outcome` kind (COR-037) from the ENGINE's fold over one declared child process's members -- both carry no predicate (the resolution / fold is the check).

kind.description: deterministic: the predicate's `result` decides. authorisation-artifact: the predicate returns {exists, produced_by}; the ENGINE computes result = exists && produced_by != actor (cross-authority). subprocess-outcome (COR-036): the ENGINE recursively resolves the embedding `subprocess` state's inner process to a terminal outcome and computes result = (inner reached the named `outcome`); no capability predicate. cascade-outcome (COR-037): the ENGINE folds the outcomes of the process's declared `cascade` child members and computes result from the reducer (all / count-against-threshold), fail-closed on any unresolved member; the DETERMINATELY-empty set is decided by the cascade's `on_empty` policy (fail-closed default / satisfied), while indeterminate membership overrides it and stays unresolved; no capability predicate and no per-gate `outcome` (the named outcome lives in the cascade reducer).

outcome.description: COR-036: for a `subprocess-outcome` gate, the inner process OUTCOME (a terminal state id of the embedded inner process) this move leaves the subprocess state on. The engine resolves the inner live and the gate passes iff the inner reached exactly this outcome. Required for `subprocess-outcome`; forbidden otherwise (enforced below).

allOf.description: A `deterministic` or `authorisation-artifact` gate REQUIRES a capability `predicate` to evaluate; a `subprocess-outcome` gate FORBIDS one (the engine computes it from the recursive resolution) and instead REQUIRES the `outcome` it tests for; a `cascade-outcome` gate (COR-037) FORBIDS both a `predicate` and a per-gate `outcome` (the engine folds the process's `cascade` declaration; the named outcome lives in the reducer).

transition.description: A guarded edge. additionalProperties permitted for capability-local fields (e.g. pr_state_effect, executor, notes).

allOf.description: A `prompt` (COR-034) is the question posed to a person, so it is meaningful only on a `user`-authorisation move (the human-pause case). It rides the existing per-move emission the status view already presents; the engine carries it content-free and never interprets it.

from.description: Source state id, or "*" for any.

to.description: Target state id. (A data-resolver target is a deferred extension.)

trigger.description: The named action that drives the move.

why.description: Status view: why this move exists.

hint.description: Status view: what to run next.

prompt.description: Optional, additive (COR-034): the QUESTION posed to the person on an `awaiting-human` wait, surfaced on this move in the status view (one additive field on the existing per-move emission). Meaningful only on a `user`-authorisation move (enforced above). The engine carries and surfaces it content-free; the binding owns what it means and what an answer does. A structured option-set (`selection`) is deferred -- render options in the prompt text until it ships.

invariant.description: A position-independent always-check (COR-035): a condition that must hold for the subject NO MATTER WHERE it sits in the process. Where detection answers 'where is this subject?' and a gate answers 'may it move here?', an invariant answers 'is something that must ALWAYS be true, true?'. The engine runs the `check` through the SAME predicate runner that backs detection and gates (single-subject, threaded with the subject id) and REPORTS the result with the `why` on failure -- it never interprets what the invariant means and never reads across subjects (COR-032). Report-only: a violation is reported by `validate` and surfaced on the status view; it does NOT block moves or remediate. NO `applies-to`/subset scoping and NO severity -- both explicitly deferred (they un-defer with composition's open region, which needs them boundary-enforcing).

id.description: Stable identifier for the invariant (reported by `validate` and on status).

check.description: The predicate the engine evaluates -- the same shape detection and gates use (a registered command returning {result, reason}). True = the invariant holds; the engine uses the predicate's own `result`. Read-only, single-subject. An indeterminate result (error / timeout / unparseable / unresolved) is fail-closed: reported as NOT holding.

why.description: Explanatory prose surfaced when the check fails -- the same `why` convention detection/gates/blocked carry. Tells a reader what is wrong and why it matters.

interface.description: COR-036: a process's PUBLIC contract for embedding -- the `inputs` it needs to start and its named `outcomes` (terminal states). A parent reads this to wire an inner process; the engine treats it as documentation of the embedding contract (it resolves outcomes from the inner's terminal states live, regardless of whether the inner declares `interface`). Optional and additive -- a process omitting it is unaffected. The child references nothing upward (coupling-in-parent): declaring an interface does not couple the child to any parent.

inputs.description: Named inputs the inner process needs to start. Content-free: the engine carries them; the parent supplies values on entry to the `subprocess` state and the inner's binding consumes them.

name.description: The input's name (the key the parent supplies on entry).

meaning.description: Load-bearing prose: what the input is for.

required.description: Whether the inner needs this input to start (default true).

outcomes.description: The inner process's named terminal states a parent may wire to its own transitions. Each names a `terminal: true` state id. Documentation of the contract -- the engine resolves the reached outcome from the inner's live terminal regardless.

name.description: A terminal state id of this process (the outcome a parent reads).

meaning.description: Load-bearing prose: what reaching this outcome means.

subprocess.description: COR-036: the embedding declaration on a `subprocess` state. It names the inner process by address, supplies the inner's inputs on entry, and (for a keyed inner) names the determinate inner subject to resolve. The engine recursively resolves the named inner process's terminal outcome (single-inner, never enumerating) and exposes it to the parent's `subprocess-outcome` gates. All coupling lives here, in the parent.

runs.description: The inner process address `<capability>:<process-id>`. The engine refuses a cyclic embedding (an address already on the active resolution stack), failing closed.

subject.description: The determinate inner subject id to resolve when the inner process is `keyed` (COR-032: a keyed inner REQUIRES a subject id; the engine errors if absent). Omitted when the inner is `singleton`. The engine NEVER enumerates the inner's subjects -- that breadth is cascade (deferred).

inputs.description: Static input values the parent supplies to the inner process on entry (keyed by the inner's `interface.inputs` names). Content-free: the engine carries them; the inner's binding consumes them. The supply mechanism beyond static values (resolving from the parent's reality) is a later extension.

cascade_reducer.description: COR-037: how the engine FOLDS the resolved outcomes of a cascade's members into one yes/no. Two operations, one enumerate-and-fold machine differing only in the reducer. `all`: every member reached the named `outcome` (grounded by pm closure + trip-planning all-verified). `count`: at least `threshold` members reached the named `outcome` (a saturation floor). Both are fail-closed on members: any member whose outcome is unresolved/indeterminate holds the fold unresolved. On a DETERMINATELY-empty set the reducer is NOT evaluated -- the cascade's `on_empty` policy decides instead (so a `count` threshold cannot open the gate by its own vacuous arithmetic), with indeterminate membership overriding it. Richer reducers (ratios / weighted / custom) stay deferred per COR-037.

op.description: all: the fold opens iff EVERY member reached the named `outcome`. count: the fold opens iff AT LEAST `threshold` members reached the named `outcome`.

outcome.description: The child process OUTCOME (a terminal state id of the named child) each member is folded against (`all` = every member at it; `count` = how many members reached it).

threshold.description: Required for `count`, forbidden for `all` (enforced below): the saturation floor -- the fold opens iff at least this many members reached the named `outcome`. The reducer is NOT evaluated on an empty set -- the cascade's `on_empty` policy decides the empty case (not this arithmetic).

allOf.description: A `count` reducer REQUIRES a `threshold` (the saturation floor); an `all` reducer FORBIDS one (it is every-member, not count-against-threshold).

cascade.description: COR-037: a parent process's declaration that it FOLDS the outcomes of ONE named child process's members into a gate. The single sanctioned, bounded crossing of COR-032's never-enumerate line: the engine reads across subjects ONLY through this one declared child relation, scoped to one parent subject, and ONLY by a capability-supplied membership predicate run one subject at a time -- never a containment tree the engine holds, never a general subject-listing API. Direction is child -> parent, coupling in the parent (the child references nothing upward and stays reusable). The `members` predicate yields the parent-scoped candidate members; the `membership` predicate confirms each candidate belongs to this parent; each confirmed member's outcome is resolved by COR-036's single-inner resolution; the `reducer` folds them; a `cascade-outcome` gate reads the fold. The optional `on_empty` policy decides a determinately-empty member set. Optional and additive -- a process omitting it is unaffected. A process declares at most one cascade.

runs.description: The one named child process address `<capability>:<process-id>` whose member outcomes are folded. Each member is resolved as a determinate single-inner resolution (COR-036) -- a keyed child member is resolved by its own subject id (the member id the predicates yield).

members.description: The candidate-set SOURCE (the seam the engine reads the set through, mirroring how detection gets its inputs -- content-free, binding-supplied). Run ONCE threaded with the PARENT subject id, it returns the parent-scoped candidate member subject ids as {members: ["id", ...]} (read live -- determinate at the instant, never a stored or open-ended global listing). The engine never enumerates the child's subjects itself; it asks the binding for this parent's candidates.

membership.description: The per-subject membership test 'does THIS subject belong to this parent?' (COR-037). Run through the existing single-subject predicate runner, one candidate at a time (threaded with the candidate's subject id), returning {result, reason}. Confirms each candidate the `members` source yielded actually belongs to this parent before its outcome is folded -- the content-free, one-subject-at-a-time crossing COR-032 holds the line at.

on_empty.description: COR-037 (amended): the binding-supplied policy for a DETERMINATELY-empty member set -- the membership evaluation completed without error and confirmed NO candidate in the relation's pool is a member. `fail-closed` (the DEFAULT when absent) keeps the gate shut (an area with zero points-of-interest is not 'fully discovered'); `satisfied` opens it ('nothing to wait on' -- project-management's childless-container closure). Either way the empty answer is DETERMINATE; the policy only chooses which way it points. A closed two-value enumeration (exactly two grounded bindings split this edge -- not an open hook). PRECEDENCE: `on_empty` applies ONLY to a determinate-empty set -- if candidate enumeration or any membership evaluation errors / is indeterminate, the fold is held UNRESOLVED regardless of `on_empty` (including under `satisfied`); a wholesale membership-read failure that confirms zero members is NOT an empty set, it is indeterminate and the gate stays shut. `on_empty` also governs the empty case for BOTH reducers (`all` and `count`): on an empty set the reducer is not evaluated, so a `count` threshold cannot open the gate by its own vacuous arithmetic. Absent on every existing cascade declaration -- those validate byte-unchanged and keep the conservative fail-closed behaviour.

process.description: A process definition. additionalProperties permitted for capability-local blocks (e.g. pm's cascade / closure_triggers / pr_state_effect) and named-deferred slots (hooks, orchestration) until they ship.

version.description: Definition version (handles definition changes under live subjects).

invariants.description: Optional, additive (COR-035): position-independent always-checks the engine runs through `validate` and surfaces on status. Each holds process-wide (across every state). Absent on every existing process -- those validate byte-unchanged. NO subset-scoping / severity (deferred).

interface.description: Optional, additive (COR-036): the process's public embedding contract ({inputs, outcomes}). Absent on every existing process -- those validate byte-unchanged.

cascade.description: Optional, additive (COR-037): the parent's child -> parent outcome-fold declaration ({runs, members, membership, reducer}), read by a `cascade-outcome` gate. Absent on every existing process -- those validate byte-unchanged. A process declares at most one cascade.

journal_entry.description: An append-only runtime record the engine writes (the memory + audit trail). Not authored by capabilities; published here so tooling can validate journals. Two kinds: a MOVE entry (the default -- a guarded move was taken; carries to/trigger/actor) and a WAIT entry (COR-034 -- a `blocked` enter/resume event, distinguished by `event`). The journal is the audit trail; the CURRENT blocked-ness is always the live evaluation (for awaiting-human, whether the pending move has been taken; for awaiting-condition, the `resume_when` predicate), authoritative over any entry here.

event.description: COR-034: present on a WAIT entry, marking entry into / resume from a blocked wait. Absent on an ordinary move entry.

blocked_on.description: COR-034: the wait reason recorded on a blocked-enter/resume entry (audit colour).

at.description: COR-034: the state id the wait sits at (the subject's position when it entered/resumed the wait).

assignee.description: COR-034: who owned the wait, if declared (audit colour).

allOf.description: A MOVE entry (no `event`) records a guarded move and must carry to/trigger/actor; a WAIT entry (COR-034) carries `event` instead.
